sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 121, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Player at rows 24-26, cols 28-31. White strip at row 27, cols 28-31.
- Plan: Try SPACE to see if it affects the right structure.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 120 actions; a strong score needs about 71 or fewer. You are 1.7x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

SPACE didn't change the board (board_changed: False). So SPACE only works when the player is above the left structure at cols 16-19. Let me go back to the left structure and try to understand what the goal is.
Let me reconsider the whole game. I've been exploring for 120 actions without completing the level. I need to figure out the goal more efficiently.

1. Player (N) can move UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT
2. SPACE toggles something when player is above cols 16-19
3. There are three structures at the bottom
4. The bottom bar seems to be a timer/progress indicator
Maybe I need to look at the initial state more carefully. Let me check the history to see what the very first frame looked like.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: SPACE only works above left structure (cols 16-19). Need to understand the goal better.
Plan: Check initial state from history to understand what changed.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check initial state
initial_seg = history[0].frame.segmentation
print("Initial nodes:")
for n in initial_seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] < 100:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  id={n['id']} color={n['color']} pixels={n['pixels']} rows=[{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}] cols=[{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}]")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see the initial state very differently:
- Player (N) was at rows 49-51, cols 32-35 (much lower on the board)
- There was a white strip at row 48, cols 32-35
- Four structures:
  1. c (charcoal) at rows 24-27, cols 44-47 with b (blue) inside at rows 25-26, cols 45-46
  2. c at rows 28-31, cols 16-19 with b at rows 29-30, cols 17-18
  3. b at rows 28-31, cols 28-39 with g (gray) bar at rows 29-30, cols 29-38
  4. c at rows 36-39, cols 32-35 with b at rows 37-38, cols 33-34
- Bottom bar: all pink (P)
So the initial state had 4 structures, not 3. The player started at rows 49-51, cols 32-35.
Currently the player is at rows 24-26, cols 28-31. The structures have changed - the structure at rows 24-27, cols 44-47 is gone, and there's now a structure at rows 28-35, cols 16-19 (c, 24 pixels) which has grown.
Wait, let me compare initial vs current:
Initial:
- c at rows 28-31, cols 16-19 (12 pixels)
- b at rows 28-31, cols 28-39 (28 pixels)
- c at rows 36-39, cols 32-35 (12 pixels)
- c at rows 24-27, cols 44-47 (12 pixels)
Current:
- c at rows 28-35, cols 16-19 (24 pixels) - grew from 12 to 24
- c at rows 28-31, cols 24-27 (12 pixels) - NEW!
- b at rows 28-31, cols 28-39 (28 pixels)
- No c at rows 36-39, cols 32-35 anymore
- No c at rows 24-27, cols 44-47 anymore
So some structures disappeared and others appeared or grew. This is very confusing. Let me look at more history to understand what happened.
Actually, let me focus on what I can control. The player started at rows 49-51, cols 32-35. The white strip was at row 48. The structure at rows 36-39, cols 32-35 was directly above the player.
Maybe the goal is to interact with each structure by positioning the player above it and pressing SPACE. Let me check what the current state of the bottom bar is - it shows P pixels=26, c pixels=38. Initially it was all P (64 pixels). So the bar has been changing.
Let me think about this differently. The bottom bar seems to track progress. Initially all pink (64 pixels). Now it's 26 pink + 38 charcoal = 64 total. So the bar fills with charcoal as progress is made.
The goal might be to fill the entire bar with charcoal. Currently 38/64 is charcoal.
Let me go back to the left structure (cols 16-19) and try SPACE there again.

seg = current_frame.segmentation

    if n['pixels'] < 100:

        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  id={n['id']} color={n['color']} pixels={n['pixels']} rows=[{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}] cols=[{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}]")
</parameter>
</function>
